headline: Indonesia's New Fishing Treaty Promises Safer Work and Fairer Pay
meta_description: Indonesia ratified the ILO Work in Fishing Convention, committing to safer conditions, fairer wages, and better healthcare for fishing crews across the industr…
summary_short: Indonesia ratifies landmark treaty to protect fishing workers with safer conditions, fairer pay, and healthcare access—closing long-standing legal gaps.
body:
Indonesia has taken a major step toward protecting its fishing workers by ratifying the International Labour Organization's Work in Fishing Convention of 2007. The ratification, completed in April, commits the government to establishing minimum standards for occupational safety, fair wages, decent working conditions, social security, and adequate rest throughout the industry. The country now has one year to revise regulations governing recruitment, training, certification, and crew management—a challenge that will test the government's resolve to close legal gaps that have left domestic fishing workers vulnerable to poor oversight and weak labor protections.

The ratification addresses a longstanding problem. Endi, a 42-year-old deckhand at Jakarta's Nizam Zachman fishing port, earns around 25,000 rupiah per day—about $1.40—for a two-month voyage totaling roughly $84 to $334. Labor unions and civil society groups argue that existing Indonesian regulations lack sufficiently detailed provisions to protect such workers. As Arnon Hiborang, chair of the Indonesian Fishing Crew Union's North Sulawesi chapter, stated: "There should be no gray areas anymore."

Beyond immediate worker welfare, experts say the treaty could deliver broader benefits. Stronger labor standards may help curb illegal fishing and boost Indonesia's seafood export credibility in international markets with strict sustainability requirements. Yet implementation remains the critical test. Researchers emphasize the need for clearer regulations for larger vessels, improved medical facilities, stronger oversight of crewing agencies, and education programs—while acknowledging that small-scale fisheries and local communities will struggle to comply without targeted government support.
quick_facts: Indonesia ratified ILO Work in Fishing Convention in April 2024
Deckhands earn approximately $1.40 per day on Jakarta fishing boats
Government has one year to update all fishing labor regulations
Convention covers safety, wages, healthcare, and rest standards
Fishing industry employs 15 times more workers than merchant shipping
